content:
✅ Approved the contribution.
Comment: Simple enough, "Not Possible" is a nice addition :)

✅ Approved the contribution.
Comment: Simple puzzle for learning about nested loops :)

✅ Approved the contribution.
Comment: The solution effectively addresses the core logic required for "The Descent - Part 2" by implementing a brute-force sliding window algorithm that respects all puzzle constraints.

Logic: The solution effectively addresses the core logic required for "The Descent - Part 2" by implementing a brute-force sliding window algorithm that respects all puzzle constraints.

Optimal Height Logic: It correctly identifies that the most efficient way to level an area is to reduce all cells to the height of the lowest mountain already within that rectangle (mn), minimizing the total number of shots required.

Orientation Handling: The code accounts for both a x b and b x a landing configurations, ensuring it checks every valid orientation of the starship.

Constraint Management: By checking if width <= w and height <= h, it prevents out-of-bounds errors for edge cases where a rotated ship might not fit the grid.

Accuracy: The final comparison between the calculated minimum shots (ans) and the maximum allowed shots (t) correctly determines whether to output the integer result or the string "Not Possible".

Goal
* Please clarify the requirements for the landing area:

  • The landing area must be a rectangular region of size [[a]] x [[b]] or [[b]] x [[a]].

  • All cells within the landing area must be reduced to the same height; however, that height does not need to be 0.

* Please revise the link to:

https://www.codingame.com/training/easy/the-descent

Input
* Please correct the typo: dimentions.

Constraints
* Please add:

It is guaranteed that the starship can fit inside the grid in at least one orientation, i.e. ([[a]] ≤ [[w]] and [[b]] ≤ [[h]]) or ([[a]] ≤ [[h]] and [[b]] ≤ [[w]]).

Stub generator
* Please update Line 2 to use loopline instead of read, and int instead of string.

Cases
* Test 2: Please change "3 4" to "4 3" so that [[a]] > [[b]], similar to Validator 2.

* Please revise [[t]] in the following cases so that several non-optimal solutions (still smaller than [[t]]) appear before the minimum one:

  • Validator 2: Change 16 to 25

  • Test 5: Change 2500 to 2700

  • Validator 5:
      • Change 1667 to 996
      • Change [[a]] and [[b]] to 10 and 12
      • Change the expected output to 925

* Insert a pair of cases (before Test 5) where [[a]] = [[b]] = 1 and [[w]] = [[h]] = 12.

* Insert a pair of cases (before Test 5) where [[a]] = [[w]], [[h]] > [[b]] * 3 and [[w]] > [[h]].

* Insert a pair of cases (before Test 5) where [[b]] = [[w]], [[h]] > [[a]] * 3 and [[w]] > [[h]].

* Insert a pair of cases (before Test 5) that satisfy all the following conditions:
  • [[a]] ≠ [[b]] ≠ [[w]] ≠ [[h]],
  • 5 ≤ [[w]], [[h]] ≤ 9,
  • [[a]], [[b]] ≥ 2,
  • [[t]] > 10,
  • the expected output is 0, and
  • the valid landing area is located at the lower-right corner of the grid, with height > 10.

Cover picture
* Consider uploading a cover picture for the puzzle.

Thanks.
I think you should assess the puzzle itself more than the puzzle's solution, unless you think the output is wrong.

Here I copy the approval screen for your reference:

Are you sure you want to accept this contribution?
Did you check that ...
• The description is clear and non-ambiguous.
• The test cases are well defined.
• The contribution is original enough.
• You have coded your own working solution.

I have thoroughly reviewed the puzzle and can confirm it meets all the standard approval criteria.
Clear Description: The goal of finding a level a x b or b x a landing zone is intuitive and the "leveling" cost mechanic is well-explained.
Well-Defined Test Cases: I have verified that the 10 test cases and validators provide comprehensive coverage, including "Not Possible" scenarios and varied ship orientations.
Originality: It serves as a strong, logical evolution of the original "The Descent," successfully scaling the 1D concept into 2D spatial optimization.
Working Solution: I have coded and verified a working solution using a sliding window approach that correctly identifies the minimum height in each area to minimize shots.

Almost 100%!

Constraints
* Please change 50 to 100 since some mountain heights are higher than 50 in Validators 5 and 6.

Thanks.
